problem: Let f(n) be the maximal k such that in any set A⊂ ℝ of size n there is a subset B⊆ A of size | B|≥ k which is dissociated that is, the sums ∑_{b∈ S}b are distinct for all S⊆ B. Estimate f(n) - in particular, is it true thatf(n)≥ \lfloor \log_2 n\rfloor?
original_latex: Let $f(n)$ be the maximal $k$ such that in any set $A\subset \mathbb{R}$ of size $n$ there is a subset $B\subseteq A$ of size $\lvert B\rvert\geq k$ which is dissociated that is, the sums $\sum_{b\in S}b$ are distinct for all $S\subseteq B$. Estimate $f(n)$ - in particular, is it true that\[f(n)\geq \lfloor \log_2 n\rfloor?\]
additional_context: Erdős noted that the greedy algorithm showed f(n)≥ \lfloor \log_3 n\rfloor.
reference_proof_hint:
Let

[
d(A):=\max{|B|:B\subseteq A\text{ is dissociated}},
\qquad
f(n):=\min_{|A|=n} d(A).
]

[[nomath]](So $f(n)$ is the **guaranteed** size of a dissociated subset in any $n$-point set.)[[/nomath]]

A finite set (B\subset \mathbb R) is dissociated iff the only relation
(\sum_{b\in B}\varepsilon_b b=0) with (\varepsilon_b\in{-1,0,1}) is the trivial one; this is equivalent to the “all subset sums (\sum_{b\in S}b) are distinct” definition.

## Unconditional lower bound: (f(n)\ge \lfloor \log_3 n\rfloor)

Take (B\subseteq A) **maximal by inclusion** among dissociated subsets of $A$.
Then for every (a\in A\setminus B), the set (B\cup{a}) is *not* dissociated, so there exist two different subsets (S,T\subseteq B\cup{a}) with equal sum, and necessarily $a$ appears on exactly one side. Rearranging gives

[
a=\sum_{b\in B}\varepsilon_b b,\qquad \varepsilon_b\in{-1,0,1}.
]

Thus every element of $A$ lies in the ({-1,0,1})-span of $B$, which contains at most (3^{|B|}) distinct coefficient choic
